text.NRSV: There was once a man in the land of Uz whose name was Job. That man was blameless and upright, one who feared God and turned away from evil.
text.Rotherham: A man, there was—in the land of Uz, Job, his name,—and that man was blameless and upright, and one who revered God, and avoided evil.

text.NRSV: There were born to him seven sons and three daughters.
text.Rotherham: And there were born unto him seven sons and three daughters.

text.NRSV: He had seven thousand sheep, three thousand camels, five hundred yoke of oxen, five hundred donkeys, and very many servants; so that this man was the greatest of all the people of the east.
text.Rotherham: And his substance was—seven thousand sheep, and three thousand camels, and five hundred yoke of oxen, and five hundred she-asses, and a body of servants exceeding large,—thus was that man the greatest of all the sons of the East.

text.NRSV: His sons used to go and hold a feast in one another’s houses in turn; and they would send and invite their three sisters to eat and drink with them.
text.Rotherham: Now his sons were wont to go, and make a banquet, at the house of each one upon his day,—and to send and call their three sisters, to eat and to drink with them.

text.NRSV: And when the feast days had run their course, Job would send and sanctify them, and he would rise early in the morning and offer burnt offerings according to the number of them all; for Job said, “It may be that my children have sinned, and cursed God in their hearts.” This is what Job always did.
text.Rotherham: And so it was, when the days of the banquet came round, that Job sent and hallowed them, and rising early in the morning offered ascending-sacrifices according to the number of them all; for Job said, Peradventure my sons have sinned, and have cursed God in their hearts. Thus and thus, was Job wont to do all the days.

text.NRSV: One day the heavenly beings came to present themselves before the Lord, and Satan also came among them.
text.Rotherham: Now there came a certain day, when the sons of God entered in to present themselves unto Yahweh,—so the accuser also entered, in their midst.

text.NRSV: The Lord said to Satan, “Where have you come from?” Satan answered the Lord, “From going to and fro on the earth, and from walking up and down on it.”
text.Rotherham: And Yahweh said unto the accuser, Whence comest thou? And the accuser answered Yahweh, and said, From going to and fro in the earth, and wandering about therein.

text.NRSV: The Lord said to Satan, “Have you considered my servant Job? There is no one like him on the earth, a blameless and upright man who fears God and turns away from evil.”
text.Rotherham: And Yahweh said unto the accuser, Hast thou applied thy heart unto my servant Job, that there is none like him in the earth, a man blameless and upright, one revering God and avoiding evil?

text.NRSV: Then Satan answered the Lord, “Does Job fear God for nothing?
text.Rotherham: And the accuser answered Yahweh, and said, Is it, for nought, that Job revereth God?

text.NRSV: Have you not put a fence around him and his house and all that he has, on every side? You have blessed the work of his hands, and his possessions have increased in the land.
text.Rotherham: Hast not, thou thyself, made a hedge about him, and about his house, and about all that he hath, on every side? The work of his hands, thou hast blessed, and, his substance, hath broken forth in the land.

text.NRSV: But stretch out your hand now, and touch all that he has, and he will curse you to your face.”
text.Rotherham: But, in very deed, put forth, I pray thee, thy hand, and smite all that he hath,—verily, unto thy face, will he curse thee.

text.NRSV: The Lord said to Satan, “Very well, all that he has is in your power; only do not stretch out your hand against him!” So Satan went out from the presence of the Lord.
text.Rotherham: And Yahweh said unto the accuser, Lo! all that he hath, is in thy hand, only, against himself, do not put forth thy hand. So the accuser went forth from the presence of Yahweh.

text.NRSV: One day when his sons and daughters were eating and drinking wine in the eldest brother’s house,
text.Rotherham: And there came a certain day,—when his sons and his daughters were eating, and drinking wine, in the house of their eldest brother.

text.NRSV: a messenger came to Job and said, “The oxen were plowing and the donkeys were feeding beside them,
text.Rotherham: And, a messenger, came in unto Job, and said,—The oxen, were plowing, and, the asses, feeding beside them;

text.NRSV: and the Sabeans fell on them and carried them off, and killed the servants with the edge of the sword; I alone have escaped to tell you.”
text.Rotherham: when the Sabeans swooped down, and took them, and, the young men, smote they with the edge of the sword,—and escaped am, only I alone, too tell thee.

text.NRSV: While he was still speaking, another came and said, “The fire of God fell from heaven and burned up the sheep and the servants, and consumed them; I alone have escaped to tell you.”
text.Rotherham: Yet was this one speaking, when, another, came in and said,—A fire of God, fell out of the heavens, and burned up the sheep and the young men, and consumed them; and escaped am, only I alone, to tell thee.

text.NRSV: While he was still speaking, another came and said, “The Chaldeans formed three columns, made a raid on the camels and carried them off, and killed the servants with the edge of the sword; I alone have escaped to tell you.”
text.Rotherham: Yet was this one speaking, when, another, came in and said, The Chaldeans, appointed three chiefs, and spread out against the camels, and took them, and, the young men, smote they with the edge of the sword; and escaped am, only I alone, to tell thee.

text.NRSV: While he was still speaking, another came and said, “Your sons and daughters were eating and drinking wine in their eldest brother’s house,
text.Rotherham: Yet was this one speaking, when, another, came in and said,—Thy sons and thy daughters, were eating, and drinking wine, in the house of their eldest brother;

text.NRSV: and suddenly a great wind came across the desert, struck the four corners of the house, and it fell on the young people, and they are dead; I alone have escaped to tell you.”
text.Rotherham: when lo! a great wind, came from over the desert, and smote the four corners of the house, and it fell upon the young men, and they died,—and escaped am, only I alone, to tell thee.

text.NRSV: Then Job arose, tore his robe, shaved his head, and fell on the ground and worshiped.
text.Rotherham: Then Job arose, and rent his robe, and shaved his head, and fell to the earth and worshipped;

text.NRSV: He said, “Naked I came from my mother’s womb, and naked shall I return; the Lord gave, and the Lord has taken away; blessed be the name of the Lord.”
text.Rotherham: and said—Naked came I forth from the womb of my mother, and naked must I return thither, Yahweh, gave, and, Yahweh, hath taken away,—The name of Yahweh be blessed!

text.NRSV: In all this Job did not sin or charge God with wrongdoing.
text.Rotherham: In all this, Job sinned not, nor imputed folly unto God.

text.NRSV: One day the heavenly beings came to present themselves before the Lord, and Satan also came among them to present himself before the Lord.
text.Rotherham: And there came a certain day when the sons of God entered in, to present themselves unto Yahweh—so the accuser also entered in their midst, to present himself unto Yahweh.

text.NRSV: The Lord said to Satan, “Where have you come from?” Satan answered the Lord, “From going to and fro on the earth, and from walking up and down on it.”
text.Rotherham: And Yahweh said unto the accuser, Whence comest thou? And the accuser answered Yahweh, and said, From going to and fro in the earth, and from wandering about therein.

text.NRSV: The Lord said to Satan, “Have you considered my servant Job? There is no one like him on the earth, a blameless and upright man who fears God and turns away from evil. He still persists in his integrity, although you incited me against him, to destroy him for no reason.”
text.Rotherham: And Yahweh said unto the accuser, Hast thou applied thy heart unto my servant Job, that there is none like him in the earth, a man blameless and upright, one who revereth God, and avoideth evil; and still he is holding fast his integrity, although thou movedst me against him, to swallow him up without cause.

text.NRSV: Then Satan answered the Lord, “Skin for skin! All that people have they will give to save their lives.
text.Rotherham: Then the accuser answered Yahweh, and said, Skin for skin, and so, all that a man hath, will he give for his life.

text.NRSV: But stretch out your hand now and touch his bone and his flesh, and he will curse you to your face.”
text.Rotherham: In very deed, put forth, I pray thee thy hand, and smite unto his bone, and unto his flesh,—verily, unto thy face, will he curse thee.

text.NRSV: The Lord said to Satan, “Very well, he is in your power; only spare his life.”
text.Rotherham: And Yahweh said unto the accuser, Behold him! in thy hand,—only, his life, preserve thou!

text.NRSV: So Satan went out from the presence of the Lord, and inflicted loathsome sores on Job from the sole of his foot to the crown of his head.
text.Rotherham: So the accuser went forth from the presence of Yahweh,—and smote Job with a sore boil, from the sole of his foot, unto his crown.

text.NRSV: Job took a potsherd with which to scrape himself, and sat among the ashes.
text.Rotherham: And he took him a potsherd, to scrape himself therewith; he being seated in the midst of ashes.

text.NRSV: Then his wife said to him, “Do you still persist in your integrity? Curse God, and die.”
text.Rotherham: Then said his wife unto him, Art thou still holding fast thine integrity? Curse God, and die!

text.NRSV: But he said to her, “You speak as any foolish woman would speak. Shall we receive the good at the hand of God, and not receive the bad?” In all this Job did not sin with his lips.
text.Rotherham: And he said unto her, As one of the base women speaketh, speakest thou? Blessing, shall we accept from God, and, misfortune, shall we not accept? In all this, Job sinned not with his lips.

text.NRSV: Now when Job’s three friends heard of all these troubles that had come upon him, each of them set out from his home—Eliphaz the Temanite, Bildad the Shuhite, and Zophar the Naamathite. They met together to go and console and comfort him.
text.Rotherham: Now when the three friends of Job heard of all this misfortune which had befallen him,—they came, every man from his own place, Eliphaz the Temanite, and Bildad the Shuhite, and Zophar the Naamathite,—for they had by appointment met together to come to shew sympathy with him, and to comfort him.

text.NRSV: When they saw him from a distance, they did not recognize him; and they raised their voices and wept aloud; they tore their robes and threw dust in the air upon their heads.
text.Rotherham: And, when they lifted up their eyes afar off, and knew him not, they lifted up their voice, and wept—and rent, every one his robe, and sprinkled dust upon their heads, toward the heavens.

text.NRSV: They sat with him on the ground seven days and seven nights, and no one spoke a word to him, for they saw that his suffering was very great.
text.Rotherham: And they sat with him upon the ground, seven days and seven nights,—and none was speaking unto him a word, for they saw that, exceeding great, was the stinging pain.

text.NRSV: After this Job opened his mouth and cursed the day of his birth.
text.Rotherham: After this, opened Job his mouth, and cursed his day.

text.NRSV: Job said:
text.Rotherham: So then Job began, and said:

text.NRSV: “Let the day perish in which I was born, and the night that said, ‘A man-child is conceived.’
text.Rotherham: Perish, the day wherein I was born, and the night it was said, Lo! a manchild!

text.NRSV: Let that day be darkness! May God above not seek it, or light shine on it.
text.Rotherham: That day, be it darkness,—Let not God enquire after it from above, May there shine upon it no clear beam:

text.NRSV: Let gloom and deep darkness claim it. Let clouds settle upon it; let the blackness of the day terrify it.
text.Rotherham: Let darkness and death-shade buy it back, May there settle down upon it a cloud, Let a day’s dark eclipse cause it terror:

text.NRSV: That night—let thick darkness seize it! Let it not rejoice among the days of the year; let it not come into the number of the months.
text.Rotherham: That night, darkness take it,—May it not rejoice among the days of the year, Into the number of months, let it not enter.

text.NRSV: Yes, let that night be barren; let no joyful voice be heard in it.
text.Rotherham: Lo! that night, be it barren, Let no joyous shouting enter therein:

text.NRSV: Let those curse it who curse the Sea, those who are skilled to rouse up Leviathan.
text.Rotherham: Let day-cursers denounce it, Those skilled in rousing the dragon of the sky:

text.NRSV: Let the stars of its dawn be dark; let it hope for light, but have none; may it not see the eyelids of the morning—
text.Rotherham: Darkened be the stars of its twilight,—Let it wait for light, and there be none, neither let it see the eyelashes of the dawn:

text.NRSV: because it did not shut the doors of my mother’s womb, and hide trouble from my eyes.
text.Rotherham: Because it closed not the doors of the womb wherein I was, and so hid trouble from mine eyes.

text.NRSV: “Why did I not die at birth, come forth from the womb and expire?
text.Rotherham: Wherefore, in the womb, did I not die? From the womb, come forth and cease to breathe?

text.NRSV: Why were there knees to receive me, or breasts for me to suck?
text.Rotherham: For what reason, were there prepared for me—knees? and why—breasts, that I might suck?

text.NRSV: Now I would be lying down and quiet; I would be asleep; then I would be at rest
text.Rotherham: Surely, at once, had I lain down, and been quiet, I had fallen asleep, then, had I been at rest:

text.NRSV: with kings and counselors of the earth who rebuild ruins for themselves,
text.Rotherham: With kings, and counselors of the earth, who had built them pyramids:

text.NRSV: or with princes who have gold, who fill their houses with silver.
text.Rotherham: Or with rulers possessing, gold,—Who had filled their houses with silver:

text.NRSV: Or why was I not buried like a stillborn child, like an infant that never sees the light?
text.Rotherham: Or that, like an untimely birth hidden away, I had not come into being, like infants that never saw light:

text.NRSV: There the wicked cease from troubling, and there the weary are at rest.
text.Rotherham: There, the lawless, cease from raging, and there the toil-worn are at rest:

text.NRSV: There the prisoners are at ease together; they do not hear the voice of the taskmaster.
text.Rotherham: At once are prisoners at peace, they hear not the voice of a driver:

text.NRSV: The small and the great are there, and the slaves are free from their masters.
text.Rotherham: Small and great, there, they are, and, the slave, is free from his master.

text.NRSV: “Why is light given to one in misery, and life to the bitter in soul,
text.Rotherham: Wherefore give, to the wretched, light? Or, life, to the embittered in soul?—

text.NRSV: who long for death, but it does not come, and dig for it more than for hidden treasures;
text.Rotherham: Who long for death, and it is not, And have digged for it, beyond hid treasures:

text.NRSV: who rejoice exceedingly, and are glad when they find the grave?
text.Rotherham: Who rejoice unto exultation, Are glad, when they can find the grave:

text.NRSV: Why is light given to one who cannot see the way, whom God has fenced in?
text.Rotherham: To a man, whose way is concealed, And GOD hath straitly enclosed him?

text.NRSV: For my sighing comes like my food, and my groanings are poured out like water.
text.Rotherham: For, in the face of my food, my sighing, cometh in, and, poured out like the water, are my groans:

text.NRSV: Truly the thing that I fear comes upon me, and what I dread befalls me.
text.Rotherham: For, a dread, I dreaded, and it hath come upon me, and, that from which I shrank, hath overtaken me.

text.NRSV: I am not at ease, nor am I quiet; I have no rest; but trouble comes.”
text.Rotherham: I was not careless, nor was I secure, nor had I settled down,—when there came—consternation!

text.NRSV: Then Eliphaz the Temanite answered:
text.Rotherham: Then responded Eliphaz the Temanite, and said:—

text.NRSV: “If one ventures a word with you, will you be offended? But who can keep from speaking?
text.Rotherham: If one attempt a word unto thee, wilt thou be impatient? But, to restrain speech, who, can endure?

text.NRSV: See, you have instructed many; you have strengthened the weak hands.
text.Rotherham: Lo! thou hast admonished many, and, slack hands, hast thou been wont to uphold:

text.NRSV: Your words have supported those who were stumbling, and you have made firm the feeble knees.
text.Rotherham: Him that was stumbling, have thy words raised up, and, sinking knees, hast thou strengthened.

text.NRSV: But now it has come to you, and you are impatient; it touches you, and you are dismayed.
text.Rotherham: But, now, it cometh upon thee, and thou despairest, It smiteth even thee, and thou art dismayed.

text.NRSV: Is not your fear of God your confidence, and the integrity of your ways your hope?
text.Rotherham: Is not, thy reverence, thy confidence? And is not, thy hope, the very integrity of thy ways?

text.NRSV: “Think now, who that was innocent ever perished? Or where were the upright cut off?
text.Rotherham: Remember, I pray thee, who, being innocent, hath perished, or when, the upright, have been cut off.

text.NRSV: As I have seen, those who plow iniquity and sow trouble reap the same.
text.Rotherham: So far as I have seen, They who plow for iniquity and sow misery, reap the same:

text.NRSV: By the breath of God they perish, and by the blast of his anger they are consumed.
text.Rotherham: By the blast of GOD, they perish, And, by the breath of his nostrils, are they consumed:

text.NRSV: The roar of the lion, the voice of the fierce lion, and the teeth of the young lions are broken.
text.Rotherham: [Notwithstanding] the roaring of the lion, and the noise of the howling lion, yet, the teeth of the fierce lions, are broken:

text.NRSV: The strong lion perishes for lack of prey, and the whelps of the lioness are scattered.
text.Rotherham: The strong lion perishing for lack of prey, Even the whelps of the lioness, are scattered.

text.NRSV: “Now a word came stealing to me, my ear received the whisper of it.
text.Rotherham: But, unto me, something was brought by stealth,—and mine ear caught a whispering of the same:

text.NRSV: Amid thoughts from visions of the night, when deep sleep falls on mortals,
text.Rotherham: When there were thoughts, from visions of the night,—When deep sleep falleth upon men,

text.NRSV: dread came upon me, and trembling, which made all my bones shake.
text.Rotherham: Dread, came upon me, and trembling, The multitude of my bones, it put in dread:

text.NRSV: A spirit passed before my face; the hair of my flesh bristled.
text.Rotherham: Then, a spirit, over my face, floated along, The hair of my flesh bristled-up:

text.NRSV: It stood still, but I could not discern its appearance. A form was before my eyes; there was silence, then I heard a voice:
text.Rotherham: It stood still, but I could not distinguish its appearance, I looked, but there was no form before mine eyes,—A whispering voice, I heard:—

text.NRSV: ‘Can mortals be righteous before God? Can human beings be pure before their Maker?
text.Rotherham: Shall, mortal man, be more just than GOD? Or a man be more pure than, his Maker?

text.NRSV: Even in his servants he puts no trust, and his angels he charges with error;
text.Rotherham: Lo! in his own servants, he trusteth not, and, his own messengers, he chargeth with error:

text.NRSV: how much more those who live in houses of clay, whose foundation is in the dust, who are crushed like a moth.
text.Rotherham: How much more the dwellers in houses of clay, which, in the dust, have their foundation, which are crushed sooner than a moth:

text.NRSV: Between morning and evening they are destroyed; they perish forever without any regarding it.
text.Rotherham: Betwixt morning and evening, are they broken in pieces, With none to save, they utterly perish:

text.NRSV: Their tent-cord is plucked up within them, is it not? They die, and that without wisdom.’
text.Rotherham: Is not their tent-rope within them, torn away? They die, disrobed of wisdom!

text.NRSV: “Call now; is there anyone who will answer you? To which of the holy ones will you turn?
text.Rotherham: Call, I pray thee—is there one to answer thee? Or, to which of the holy ones, wilt thou turn?

text.NRSV: Surely vexation kills the fool, and passion slays the simple.
text.Rotherham: For, to the foolish man, death is caused by vexation, and, the simple one, is slain by jealousy.

text.NRSV: I have seen fools taking root, but suddenly I cursed their dwelling.
text.Rotherham: I, have seen the foolish taking root, and then hath his home decayed, in a moment:

text.NRSV: Their children are far from rather safety, they are crushed in the gate, and there is no one to deliver them.
text.Rotherham: His children are far removed from safety, and they are crushed in the gate, and there is none to deliver:

text.NRSV: The hungry eat their harvest, and they take it even out of the thorns; and the thirsty pant after their wealth.
text.Rotherham: Whose harvest, the hungry, eateth up, and, even out of thorn hedges, he taketh it, and the snare gapeth for their substance.

text.NRSV: For misery does not come from the dust, nor does trouble sprout from the ground;
text.Rotherham: For sorrow, cometh not forth out of the dust,—nor, out of the ground, sprouteth trouble.

text.NRSV: but mortals are born to trouble as the sparks fly upward.
text.Rotherham: Though, man, to trouble, were born, as, sparks, on high, do soar,

text.NRSV: “As for me, I would seek God, and to God I would commit my cause.
text.Rotherham: Yet indeed, I, would seek unto El, and, unto Elohim, would I set forth any cause:—

text.NRSV: He does great things and unsearchable, marvelous things without number.
text.Rotherham: Who doeth great things, beyond all search,—Wondrous things, till they cannot be recounted;

text.NRSV: He gives rain on the earth and sends waters on the fields;
text.Rotherham: Who giveth rain, upon the face of the earth, and sendeth forth waters, over the face of the open fields;

text.NRSV: he sets on high those who are lowly, and those who mourn are lifted to safety.
text.Rotherham: Setting the lowly on high, and, mourners, are uplifted to safety;

text.NRSV: He frustrates the devices of the crafty, so that their hands achieve no success.
text.Rotherham: Who doth frustrate the schemes of the crafty, that their hands cannot achieve abiding success;

text.NRSV: He takes the wise in their own craftiness; and the schemes of the wily are brought to a quick end.
text.Rotherham: Who captureth the wise in their own craftiness, yea the headlong counsel of the crooked:

text.NRSV: They meet with darkness in the daytime, and grope at noonday as in the night.
text.Rotherham: By day, they encounter darkness, and, as though it were night, they grope at high noon.

text.NRSV: But he saves the needy from the sword of their mouth, from the hand of the mighty.
text.Rotherham: But he saveth from the sword, out of their mouth, and, out of the hand of the strong, the needy.

text.NRSV: So the poor have hope, and injustice shuts its mouth.
text.Rotherham: Thus to the poor hath come hope, and, perversity, hath shut her mouth.

text.NRSV: “How happy is the one whom God reproves; therefore do not despise the discipline of the Almighty.
text.Rotherham: Lo! how happy is the man whom God correcteth! Therefore, the chastening of the Almighty, do not thou refuse;

text.NRSV: For he wounds, but he binds up; he strikes, but his hands heal.
text.Rotherham: For, he, woundeth that he may bind up, He smiteth through, that, his own hands, may heal.

text.NRSV: He will deliver you from six troubles; in seven no harm shall touch you.
text.Rotherham: In six troubles, he will rescue thee, and, in seven, there shall smite thee no misfortune:

text.NRSV: In famine he will redeem you from death, and in war from the power of the sword.
text.Rotherham: In famine, he will ransom thee from death, and in battle from the power of the sword;

text.NRSV: You shall be hidden from the scourge of the tongue, and shall not fear destruction when it comes.
text.Rotherham: During the scourge of the tongue, shalt thou be hid, neither shalt thou be afraid of destruction when it cometh;

text.NRSV: At destruction and famine you shall laugh, and shall not fear the wild animals of the earth.
text.Rotherham: At destruction and at hunger, shalt thou laugh, and, of the wild beast of the earth, be not thou afraid;

text.NRSV: For you shall be in league with the stones of the field, and the wild animals shall be at peace with you.
text.Rotherham: For, with the stones of the field, shall be thy covenant, and, the wild beast of the field, hath been made thy friend;

text.NRSV: You shall know that your tent is safe, you shall inspect your fold and miss nothing.
text.Rotherham: And thou shalt know that, at peace, is thy tent, and shalt visit thy fold, and miss nothing;

text.NRSV: You shall know that your descendants will be many, and your offspring like the grass of the earth.
text.Rotherham: And thou shalt know, that numerous is thy seed, and, thine offspring, like the young shoots of the field.

text.NRSV: You shall come to your grave in ripe old age, as a shock of grain comes up to the threshing floor in its season.
text.Rotherham: Thou shalt come, yet robust, to the grave, as a stack of sheaves mounteth up in its season.

text.NRSV: See, we have searched this out; it is true. Hear, and know it for yourself.”
text.Rotherham: Lo! as for this, we have searched it out—so, it is, Hear it, and know, thou, for thyself.

text.NRSV: Then Job answered:
text.Rotherham: Then responded Job, and said:—

text.NRSV: “O that my vexation were weighed, and all my calamity laid in the balances!
text.Rotherham: Oh that, weighed, were my vexation, and, my engulfing ruin—into the balances, they would lift up all at once!

text.NRSV: For then it would be heavier than the sand of the sea; therefore my words have been rash.
text.Rotherham: For, now, beyond the sand of the seas, would it be heavy, On this account, my words, have wandered.

text.NRSV: For the arrows of the Almighty are in me; my spirit drinks their poison; the terrors of God are arrayed against me.
text.Rotherham: For, the arrows of the Almighty, are in me, The heat whereof, my spirit is drinking up, The, terrors of GOD, array themselves against me.

text.NRSV: Does the wild donkey bray when it has grass, or the ox low over its fodder?
text.Rotherham: Doth the wild ass bray over grass? Or loweth the ox over his fodder?

text.NRSV: Can that which is tasteless be eaten without salt, or is there any flavor in the white of an egg?
text.Rotherham: Can that which hath no savour be eaten without salt? Or is there any taste in the white of an egg?

text.NRSV: My appetite refuses to touch them; they are like food that is loathsome to me.
text.Rotherham: My soul hath refused to touch, Those things, are like disease in my food.

text.NRSV: “O that I might have my request, and that God would grant my desire;
text.Rotherham: Oh that my request would come! and, my hope, oh that GOD would grant!

text.NRSV: that it would please God to crush me, that he would let loose his hand and cut me off!
text.Rotherham: That it would please GOD to crush me, That he would set free his hand, and cut me off!

text.NRSV: This would be my consolation; I would even exult in unrelenting pain; for I have not denied the words of the Holy One.
text.Rotherham: So might it still be my comfort, And I might exult in the anguish he would not spare,—That I had not concealed the sayings of the Holy One.

text.NRSV: What is my strength, that I should wait? And what is my end, that I should be patient?
text.Rotherham: What is my strength, that I should hope? Or what mine end, that I should prolong my desire?

text.NRSV: Is my strength the strength of stones, or is my flesh bronze?
text.Rotherham: Is my strength, the strength of stones? Or is, my flesh, of bronze?

text.NRSV: In truth I have no help in me, and any resource is driven from me.
text.Rotherham: Is there any help at all in me? Is not, abiding success, driven from me?

text.NRSV: “Those who withhold kindness from a friend forsake the fear of the Almighty.
text.Rotherham: The despairing, from his friend, should have lovingkindness, or, the reverence of the Almighty, he may forsake.

text.NRSV: My companions are treacherous like a wadi, like bed of torrents that run dry,
text.Rotherham: Mine own brethren, have proved treacherous like a torrent, like a channel of torrents which disappear:

text.NRSV: which are turbid with ice, and into which the snow vanishes.
text.Rotherham: Which darken by reason of the cold, over them, is a covering made by the snow:

text.NRSV: In time of heat they disappear; when it is hot, they vanish from their place.
text.Rotherham: By the time they begin to thaw, they are dried up, as soon as it is warm, they have vanished out of their place.

text.NRSV: The caravans turn aside from their course; they go up into the waste, and perish.
text.Rotherham: Caravans turn aside by their course, they go up into a waste, and are lost:

text.NRSV: The caravans of Tema look, the travelers of Sheba hope.
text.Rotherham: The caravans of Tema looked about, the travelling companies of Sheba, hoped for them:

text.NRSV: They are disappointed because they were confident; they come there and are confounded.
text.Rotherham: They are ashamed that they had trusted, They have come up to one of them, and are confounded.

text.NRSV: Truly you have now become a nothing; you see my calamity, and are afraid.
text.Rotherham: For, now, ye have come to him, ye see something fearful, and fear.

text.NRSV: Have I said, ‘Make me a gift’? Or, ‘From your wealth offer a bribe for me’?
text.Rotherham: Is it that I said, Make me a gift, or, out of your abundance, offer a bribe on my behalf;

text.NRSV: Or, ‘Deliver me from the adversary’s hand’? Or, ‘Redeem me from the hand of ruthless men’?
text.Rotherham: And deliver me from the hand of the adversary? And, out of the hand of tyrants, ransom me?

text.NRSV: “Teach me, and I will be silent; make me understand how I have gone wrong.
text.Rotherham: Show me, and, I, will hold my peace, And, wherein I have erred, cause me to understand.

text.NRSV: How forceful are honest words! But what does reproduction by you reprove?
text.Rotherham: How pleasant are the sayings that are right! But what can a decision from you, decide?

text.NRSV: Do you imagine that you can reprove words, when the speech of the despairing is wind?
text.Rotherham: To decide words, do ye intend, When, to the wind, are spoken the sayings of one in despair?

text.NRSV: You would even cast lots over the orphan, and bargain over your friend.
text.Rotherham: Surely, the fatherless, ye would assail, and make merchandise of your friend!

text.NRSV: “But now, be pleased to look at me; for I will not lie to your face.
text.Rotherham: But, now, be pleased to turn to me, that it may be, to your faces, if I speak falsehood,

text.NRSV: Turn, I pray, let no wrong be done. Turn now, my vindication is at stake.
text.Rotherham: Reply, I pray you, let there be no perversity, Yea reply even yet, my vindication is in it!

text.NRSV: Is there any wrong on my tongue? Cannot my taste discern calamity?
text.Rotherham: Is there, in my tongue, perversity? Or can, my sense, not discern, engulfing ruin?

text.NRSV: “Do not mortals have a hard service on earth, and are not their days like the days of a laborer?
text.Rotherham: Is there not a warfare to a mortal, upon earth? And, as the days of a hireling, are not his days?

text.NRSV: Like a slave who longs for the shadow, and like laborers who look for their wages,
text.Rotherham: As, a bondman, panteth for the shadow, and as, a hireling, longeth for his wage,

text.NRSV: so I am allotted months of emptiness, and nights of misery are appointed to me.
text.Rotherham: So, have I been made to inherit months of calamity, and, nights of weariness, have been appointed me.

text.NRSV: When I lie down I say, ‘When shall I rise?’ But the night is long, and I am full of tossing until dawn.
text.Rotherham: As soon as I lie down, I say, When shall I arise? yet he lengtheneth out the evening, and I am wearied with tossings until the breeze of twilight.

text.NRSV: My flesh is clothed with worms and dirt; my skin hardens, then breaks out afresh.
text.Rotherham: My flesh is clothed with worms and a coating of dust, My skin, hath hardened, and then run afresh:

text.NRSV: My days are swifter than a weaver’s shuttle, and come to their end without hope.
text.Rotherham: My days, are swifter than a weaver’s shuttle, and they are spent, without hope.

text.NRSV: “Remember that my life is a breath; my eye will never again see good.
text.Rotherham: Remember thou, that, a wind, is my life, not again shall mine eye see blessing:

text.NRSV: The eye that beholds me will see me no more; while your eyes are upon me, I shall be gone.
text.Rotherham: Nor shall see me—the eye that used to behold me, Thine eyes, are upon me, and I am not.

text.NRSV: As the cloud fades and vanishes, so those who go down to Sheol do not come up;
text.Rotherham: A cloud faileth, and is gone, So, he that descendeth to hades, shall not come up:

text.NRSV: they return no more to their houses, nor do their places know them any more.
text.Rotherham: He shall not return again to his house, and his own place shall be acquainted with him no more.

text.NRSV: “Therefore I will not restrain my mouth; I will speak in the anguish of my spirit; I will complain in the bitterness of my soul.
text.Rotherham: I also, cannot restrain my mouth,—I must speak, in the anguish of my spirit, I must find utterance, in the bitterness of my soul.

text.NRSV: Am I the Sea, or the Dragon, that you set a guard over me?
text.Rotherham: Am, I, a sea, or a sea-monster,—That thou shouldst set over me a watch?

text.NRSV: When I say, ‘My bed will comfort me, my couch will ease my complaint,’
text.Rotherham: When I say, My bed shall comfort me, my couch shall help to carry my complaint,

text.NRSV: then you scare me with dreams and terrify me with visions,
text.Rotherham: Then thou scarest me with dreams, and, by visions, dost thou terrify me:

text.NRSV: so that I would choose strangling and death rather than my body.
text.Rotherham: So that my soul chooseth strangling, Death, rather than [these] my bones!

text.NRSV: I loathe my life; I would not live forever. Let me alone, for my days are a breath.
text.Rotherham: I am wasted away, Not, to times age-abiding, can I live, Let me alone, for, a breath, are my days.

text.NRSV: What are human beings, that you make so much of them, that you set your mind on them,
text.Rotherham: What is a mortal, that thou shouldst nurture him? Or that thou shouldst fix upon him thy mind?

text.NRSV: visit them every morning, and test them every moment?
text.Rotherham: That thou shouldst inspect him morning by morning, moment by moment, shouldst test him?

text.NRSV: Will you not look away from me for a while, let me alone until I swallow my spittle?
text.Rotherham: How long wilt thou not look away from me? Wilt thou not let me alone, till I can swallow my spittle?

text.NRSV: If I sin, what do I do to you, you watcher of humanity? Why have you made me your target? Why have I become a burden to you?
text.Rotherham: I have sinned, What can I do for thee, thou watcher of men? Wherefore hast thou set me as thine object of attack, or have I become, unto thee, a burden?

text.NRSV: Why do you not pardon my transgression and take away my iniquity? For now I shall lie in the earth; you will seek me, but I shall not be.”
text.Rotherham: And why wilt thou not remove my transgression, and take away mine iniquity? For, now, in the dust, should I lie down, and thou shouldst seek me diligently, and I should not be.

text.NRSV: Then Bildad the Shuhite answered:
text.Rotherham: Then responded Bildad the Shuhite, and said:—

text.NRSV: “How long will you say these things, and the words of your mouth be a great wind?
text.Rotherham: How long wilt thou speak these things? Or, as a mighty wind, shall be the sayings of thy mouth?

text.NRSV: Does God pervert justice? Or does the Almighty pervert the right?
text.Rotherham: Should, GOD, pervert justice? Or, the Almighty, pervert righteousness?

text.NRSV: If your children sinned against him, he delivered them into the power of their transgression.
text.Rotherham: Though, thy children, sinned against him, and he delivered them into the hand of their transgression,

text.NRSV: If you will seek God and make supplication to the Almighty,
text.Rotherham: Yet, if, thou thyself, wilt diligently seek unto GOD,—and, unto the Almighty, wilt make supplication;

text.NRSV: if you are pure and upright, surely then he will rouse himself for you and restore to you your rightful place.
text.Rotherham: If, pure and upright, thou thyself, art, surely, now, will he answer thy prayer, and will prosper thy righteous habitation:

text.NRSV: Though your beginning was small, your latter days will be very great.
text.Rotherham: So shall thy beginning appear small,—when, thy latter end, he shall greatly increase!

text.NRSV: “For inquire now of bygone generations, and consider what their ancestors have found;
text.Rotherham: For inquire, I pray thee, of a former generation, and prepare thyself for the research of their fathers;—

text.NRSV: for we are but of yesterday, and we know nothing, for our days on earth are a shadow.
text.Rotherham: For, of yesterday, are, we, and cannot know, for, a shadow, are our days upon earth:

text.NRSV: Will they not teach you and tell you and utter words out of their understanding?
text.Rotherham: Shall, they, not teach thee—tell thee, and, out of their memory, bring forth words?

text.NRSV: “Can papyrus grow where there is no marsh? Can reeds flourish where there is no water?
text.Rotherham: Can the paper-reed grow up, without a marsh? Or the rush grow up, without water?

text.NRSV: While yet in flower and not cut down, they wither before any other plant.
text.Rotherham: Though while still, in its freshness, it be not plucked off, yet, before any kind of grass, it doth wither:

text.NRSV: Such are the paths of all who forget God; the hope of the godless shall perish.
text.Rotherham: So, shall be the latter end of all who forget GOD, and, the hope of the impious, shall perish:

text.NRSV: Their confidence is gossamer, a spider’s house their trust.
text.Rotherham: Whose trust shall be contemptible,—and, a spider’s web, his confidence:

text.NRSV: If one leans against their house, it will not stand; if one holds fast to it, it will not endure.
text.Rotherham: He leaneth upon his house, and it will not stand, he holdeth it fast, and it will not remain erect.

text.NRSV: They thrive before the sun, and their shoots spread over the garden.
text.Rotherham: Full of moisture he is, before the sun, and, over his garden, his shoot goeth forth:

text.NRSV: Their roots twine around the stoneheap; they live among the rocks.
text.Rotherham: Over a heap, his roots are entwined, a place of stones, he descrieth;

text.NRSV: If they are destroyed from their place, then it will deny them, saying, ‘I have never seen you.’
text.Rotherham: If one destroy him out of his place, then will it disown him [saying] —I have not seen thee.

text.NRSV: See, these are its joys; from the dust others will sprout.
text.Rotherham: Lo! that, is the joy of his way,—and, out of the dust, shall others spring up.

text.NRSV: “See, God will not reject a blameless person, nor take the hand of evildoers.
text.Rotherham: Lo! GOD, will not reject a blameless man, neither will he grasp the hand of evil-doers:

text.NRSV: He will yet fill your mouth with laughter, and your lips with shouts of joy.
text.Rotherham: At length he shall fill with laughter thy mouth, and thy lips, with a shout of triumph:

text.NRSV: Those who hate you will be clothed with shame, and the tent of the wicked will be no more.”
text.Rotherham: They who hate thee, shall be clothed with shame, but, the tent of the lawless, shall not be!

text.NRSV: Then Job answered:
text.Rotherham: Then responded Job, and said—

text.NRSV: “Indeed I know that this is so; but how can a mortal be just before God?
text.Rotherham: Of a truth, I know that so it is, But how can a mortal be just with GOD?

text.NRSV: If one wished to contend with him, one could not answer him once in a thousand.
text.Rotherham: If he choose to contend with him, he cannot answer him, one of a thousand:

text.NRSV: He is wise in heart, and mighty in strength—who has resisted him, and succeeded?—
text.Rotherham: Wise in heart, and alert in vigour, What man hath hardened himself against him, and prospered!

text.NRSV: he who removes mountains, and they do not know it, when he overturns them in his anger;
text.Rotherham: Who removeth mountains, unawares, Who overturneth them in his anger;

text.NRSV: who shakes the earth out of its place, and its pillars tremble;
text.Rotherham: Who shaketh the earth, out of its place, and, the pillars thereof, shudder;

text.NRSV: who commands the sun, and it does not rise; who seals up the stars;
text.Rotherham: Who commandeth the sun, and it breaketh not forth, and, about the stars, he putteth a seal;

text.NRSV: who alone stretched out the heavens and trampled the waves of the Sea;
text.Rotherham: Who spreadeth out fire heavens, by himself alone! and marcheth along, on the heights of the sea;

text.NRSV: who made the Bear and Orion, the Pleiades and the chambers of the south;
text.Rotherham: Who made the Bear, the Giant and the Cluster, and the chambers of the south;

text.NRSV: who does great things beyond understanding, and marvelous things without number.
text.Rotherham: Who doeth great things, past finding out, and marvels, beyond number.

text.NRSV: Look, he passes by me, and I do not see him; he moves on, but I do not perceive him.
text.Rotherham: Lo! he cometh upon me, yet can I not see him, Yea he passeth on, yet can I not discern him.

text.NRSV: He snatches away; who can stop him? Who will say to him, ‘What are you doing?’
text.Rotherham: Lo! he snatcheth away, who can bring it back? Who shall say unto him, What wouldst thou do?

text.NRSV: “God will not turn back his anger; the helpers of Rahab bowed beneath him.
text.Rotherham: As for GOD, if he withdraw not his anger, under him, will have submitted themselves—the proud helpers.

text.NRSV: How then can I answer him, choosing my words with him?
text.Rotherham: How much less that, I, should answer him, should choose my words with him?

text.NRSV: Though I am innocent, I cannot answer him; I must appeal for mercy to my accuser.
text.Rotherham: Whom, though I were righteous, yet would I not answer, to be absolved, I would make supplication.

text.NRSV: If I summoned him and he answered me, I do not believe that he would listen to my voice.
text.Rotherham: Though I had called, and he had answered me, I could not believe, that he would lend an ear to my voice.

text.NRSV: For he crushes me with a tempest, and multiplies my wounds without cause;
text.Rotherham: For, with a tempest, would he fall upon me, and would multiply my wounds without need;

text.NRSV: he will not let me get my breath, but fills me with bitterness.
text.Rotherham: He would not suffer me to recover my breath, for he would surfeit me with bitter things.

text.NRSV: If it is a contest of strength, he is the strong one! If it is a matter of justice, who can summon him?
text.Rotherham: If it regardeth vigour, bold is he! If justice, who could summon him?

text.NRSV: Though I am innocent, my own mouth would condemn me; though I am blameless, he would prove me perverse.
text.Rotherham: If I should justify myself, mine own mouth, would condemn me,—I blameless? then had it shewn me perverse.

text.NRSV: I am blameless; I do not know myself; I loathe my life.
text.Rotherham: I blameless? I should not know my own soul, I should despise my own life!

text.NRSV: It is all one; therefore I say, he destroys both the blameless and the wicked.
text.Rotherham: One thing, there is, for which cause, I have said it, The blameless and the lawless, he bringeth to an end.

text.NRSV: When disaster brings sudden death, he mocks at the calamity of the innocent.
text.Rotherham: If, a scourge, slay suddenly, at the despair of innocent ones, he mocketh.

text.NRSV: The earth is given into the hand of the wicked; he covers the eyes of its judges—if it is not he, who then is it?
text.Rotherham: The earth, hath been given into the hand of a lawless one, The faces of her judges, he covereth, If not, then who is it?

text.NRSV: “My days are swifter than a runner; they flee away, they see no good.
text.Rotherham: My days, therefore, are swifter than a runner, They have fled, they have seen no good.

text.NRSV: They go by like skiffs of reed, like an eagle swooping on the prey.
text.Rotherham: They have passed away with boats of paper-reed, like a vulture [which] rusheth upon food.

text.NRSV: If I say, ‘I will forget my complaint; I will put off my sad countenance and be cheerful,’
text.Rotherham: If I say, I will forget my complaint, I will lay aside my sad countenance, and brighten up,

text.NRSV: I become afraid of all my suffering, for I know you will not hold me innocent.
text.Rotherham: I am afraid of all my pains, I know, that thou wilt not pronounce me innocent.

text.NRSV: I shall be condemned; why then do I labor in vain?
text.Rotherham: I, shall be held guilty,—Wherefore then, in vain, should I toil?

text.NRSV: If I wash myself with soap and cleanse my hands with lye,
text.Rotherham: Though I bathe myself in snow water, and cleanse, in cleanness itself, my hands,

text.NRSV: yet you will plunge me into filth, and my own clothes will abhor me.
text.Rotherham: Then, in a ditch, wouldst thou plunge me, and mine own clothes should abhor me:

text.NRSV: For he is not a mortal, as I am, that I might answer him, that we should come to trial together.
text.Rotherham: For he is not a man like myself, whom I might answer, nor could we come together into judgment:

text.NRSV: There is no umpire between us, who might lay his hand on us both.
text.Rotherham: There is not, between us, a mediator, who might lay his hand upon us both.

text.NRSV: Let him take his rod away from me, and let not dread of him terrify me.
text.Rotherham: Let him take from off me his rod, and, his terror, let it not startle me:

text.NRSV: Then I would speak without fear of him, for I know I am not what I am thought to be.
text.Rotherham: I could speak, and not be afraid of him, although, not so, am, I, in myself!

text.NRSV: “I loathe my life; I will give free utterance to my complaint; I will speak in the bitterness of my soul.
text.Rotherham: My soul doth loathe my life,—I let loose my complaint, I speak, in the bitterness of my soul.

text.NRSV: I will say to God, Do not condemn me; let me know why you contend with me.
text.Rotherham: I say unto GOD, Do not hold me guilty, Let me know, on what account thou contendest with me!

text.NRSV: Does it seem good to you to oppress, to despise the work of your hands and favor the schemes of the wicked?
text.Rotherham: Is it seemly in thee, that thou shouldst oppress? that thou shouldst despise the labour of thine own hand, when, upon the counsel of the lawless, thou hast shone?

text.NRSV: Do you have eyes of flesh? Do you see as humans see?
text.Rotherham: Eyes of flesh, hast thou? or, as a mortal seeth, seest thou?

text.NRSV: Are your days like the days of mortals, or your years like human years,
text.Rotherham: As the days of a mortal, are thy days? or, thy years, as the days of a man?

text.NRSV: that you seek out my iniquity and search for my sin,
text.Rotherham: That thou shouldst seek for mine iniquity, and, for my sin, shouldst make search:

text.NRSV: although you know that I am not guilty, and there is no one to deliver out of your hand?
text.Rotherham: Though it is, within thine own knowledge, that I would not be lawless, and, none, out of thy hand, can deliver?

text.NRSV: Your hands fashioned and made me; and now you turn and destroy me.
text.Rotherham: Thine own hands, shaped me, and made me, all in unison round about, and yet thou hast confounded me.

text.NRSV: Remember that you fashioned me like clay; and will you turn me to dust again?
text.Rotherham: Remember, I pray thee, that, as clay, thou didst make me, and, unto dust, thou wilt cause me to return.

text.NRSV: Did you not pour me out like milk and curdle me like cheese?
text.Rotherham: Didst thou not, like milk, pour me forth? and, as cheese, curdle me?

text.NRSV: You clothed me with skin and flesh, and knit me together with bones and sinews.
text.Rotherham: With skin and flesh, clothe me? and, with bones and sinews, interweave me?

text.NRSV: You have granted me life and steadfast love, and your care has preserved my spirit.
text.Rotherham: Life and lovingkindness, thou didst bestow upon me,—and, thy watchful care, preserved my breath.

text.NRSV: Yet these things you hid in your heart; I know that this was your purpose.
text.Rotherham: Yet, these things, thou didst hide in thy heart, I know that, this, hath been with thee!

text.NRSV: If I sin, you watch me, and do not acquit me of my iniquity.
text.Rotherham: If I have sinned, then couldst thou watch me, and, from mine iniquity, thou wouldst not acquit me:

text.NRSV: If I am wicked, woe to me! If I am righteous, I cannot lift up my head, for I am filled with disgrace and look upon my misery.
text.Rotherham: If I have been lawless, alas for me! Or, if I am righteous, I will not lift up my head, Surfeited with shame, look thou then on my humiliation.

text.NRSV: Bold as a lion you hunt me; you repeat your exploits against me.
text.Rotherham: When it is lifted up, like a howling lion, thou dost hunt me, Then again thou dost shew thyself marvellous against me.

text.NRSV: You renew your witnesses against me, and increase your vexation toward me; you bring fresh troops against me.
text.Rotherham: Thou renewest thy witnesses before me, and dost increase thy vexation with me, Relays—yea an army, is with me.

text.NRSV: “Why did you bring me forth from the womb? Would that I had died before any eye had seen me,
text.Rotherham: Wherefore then, from the womb, didst thou bring me forth? I might have breathed my last, and, no eye, have seen me.

text.NRSV: and were as though I had not been, carried from the womb to the grave.
text.Rotherham: As though I had not been, should I have become,—from the womb to the grave, might I have been borne.

text.NRSV: Are not the days of my life few? Let me alone, that I may find a little comfort
text.Rotherham: Are not my days, few?—then forbear, and set me aside, that I may brighten up for a little;

text.NRSV: before I go, never to return, to the land of gloom and deep darkness,
text.Rotherham: Before I go, and not return, unto a land of darkness and death-shade:

text.NRSV: the land of utter gloom and chaos, where light is as darkness.”
text.Rotherham: A land of obscurity, like thick darkness, of death-shade and disorder, and which shineth like thick darkness.

text.NRSV: Then Zophar the Naamathite answered:
text.Rotherham: Then responded Zophar the Naamathite, and said:—

text.NRSV: “Should a multitude of words go unanswered, and should one full of talk be vindicated?
text.Rotherham: Should, the multitude of words, not be answered? Or should, a man full of talk, be justified?

text.NRSV: Should your babble put others to silence, and when you mock, shall no one shame you?
text.Rotherham: Shall, thy pratings, cause men to hold their peace? When thou hast mocked, shall there be none to put thee to shame?

text.NRSV: For you say, ‘My conduct is pure, and I am clean in God’s sight.’
text.Rotherham: Since thou hast said, Right is my doctrine, and pure am I in his eyes.

text.NRSV: But O that God would speak, and open his lips to you,
text.Rotherham: But, in very deed, oh that GOD would speak, that he would open his lips with thee:

text.NRSV: and that he would tell you the secrets of wisdom! For wisdom is many-sided. Know then that God exacts of you less than your guilt deserves.
text.Rotherham: That he would declare to thee the secrets of wisdom, for they are double to that which actually is,—Know then that GOD could bring into forgetfulness for thee, a portion of thine iniquity.

text.NRSV: “Can you find out the deep things of God? Can you find out the limit of the Almighty?
text.Rotherham: The hidden depth of GOD canst thou discover? Or, unto the furthest limit of the Almighty, canst thou attain?

text.NRSV: It is higher than heaven—what can you do? Deeper than Sheol—what can you know?
text.Rotherham: The heights of the heavens, what canst thou do? Depths deeper than hades, what canst thou know?

text.NRSV: Its measure is longer than the earth, and broader than the sea.
text.Rotherham: Longer than the earth, is the measure thereof, and broader than the sea.

text.NRSV: If he passes through, and imprisons, and assembles for judgment, who can hinder him?
text.Rotherham: If he sweep on, or shut up, or call together, Who then shall hinder him?

text.NRSV: For he knows those who are worthless; when he sees iniquity, will he not consider it?
text.Rotherham: For, he, knoweth men of falsity, and seeth iniquity, and him that doth not diligently consider.

text.NRSV: But a stupid person will get understanding, when a wild donkey is born human.
text.Rotherham: But, an empty person, will get sense, when, a wild ass’s colt, is born a man!

text.NRSV: “If you direct your heart rightly, you will stretch out your hands toward him.
text.Rotherham: If, thou, hast prepared thy heart, and wilt spread forth, unto him, thy hands—

text.NRSV: If iniquity is in your hand, put it far away, and do not let wickedness reside in your tents.
text.Rotherham: If, iniquity, be in thy hand, Put it far away, and let there not dwell in thy tents perversity,

text.NRSV: Surely then you will lift up your face without blemish; you will be secure, and will not fear.
text.Rotherham: Surely, then, shalt thou lift up thy face free from blemish, and shalt be established, and not fear.

text.NRSV: You will forget your misery; you will remember it as waters that have passed away.
text.Rotherham: For, now, shalt thou forget, sorrow, Like waters passed away, shalt thou remember it.

text.NRSV: And your life will be brighter than the noonday; its darkness will be like the morning.
text.Rotherham: Above high noon, shall rise life’s continuance, Darkness, like a morning, shall appear,

text.NRSV: And you will have confidence, because there is hope; you will be protected and take your rest in safety.
text.Rotherham: And thou shalt he confident, that there is hope, and, when thou hast searched, securely shalt thou lie down;

text.NRSV: You will lie down, and no one will make you afraid; many will entreat your favor.
text.Rotherham: And shalt rest, with none to put thee in terror,—and many shall entreat thy favour.

text.NRSV: But the eyes of the wicked will fail; all way of escape will be lost to them, and their hope is to breathe their last.”
text.Rotherham: But, the eyes of the lawless, shall fail,—and, place of refuge, shall have vanished from them, and, their hope, be a breathing out of life.

text.NRSV: Then Job answered:
text.Rotherham: Then responded Job, and said:—

text.NRSV: “No doubt you are the people, and wisdom will die with you.
text.Rotherham: Of a truth, ye, are the people, and, with you, wisdom, will die.

text.NRSV: But I have understanding as well as you; I am not inferior to you. Who does not know such things as these?
text.Rotherham: I also, have a mind like you, I, fall not short, of you, But who hath not such things as these?

text.NRSV: I am a laughingstock to my friends; I, who called upon God and he answered me, a just and blameless man, I am a laughingstock.
text.Rotherham: A laughing-stock to one’s neighbour, do I become, one who hath called upon GOD and he hath answered him! A laughing-stock—a righteous man without blame!

text.NRSV: Those at ease have contempt for misfortune, but it is ready for those whose feet are unstable.
text.Rotherham: For ruin, there is contempt, in the thought of the man at ease,—ready, for such as are of faltering foot!

text.NRSV: The tents of robbers are at peace, and those who provoke God are secure, who bring their god in their hands.
text.Rotherham: At peace are the tents that belong to the spoilers, and there is security to them who provoke GOD, To him who bringeth a god in his hand.

text.NRSV: “But ask the animals, and they will teach you; the birds of the air, and they will tell you;
text.Rotherham: But, in very deed, ask, I pray thee, the beasts, and they will teach thee, and the bird of the heavens, and it will tell thee;

text.NRSV: or ask the plants of the earth, and they will teach you; and the fish of the sea will declare to you.
text.Rotherham: Or address the earth, and it will teach thee, and the fishes of the sea, will recount it to thee:

text.NRSV: Who among all these does not know that the hand of the Lord has done this?
text.Rotherham: Who knoweth not, among all these, that, the hand of Yahweh, hath done this?

text.NRSV: In his hand is the life of every living thing and the breath of every human being.
text.Rotherham: In whose hand is the soul of every living thing, and the spirit of all the flesh of men.

text.NRSV: Does not the ear test words as the palate tastes food?
text.Rotherham: Doth not, the ear, try, words? even as, the palate, tasteth for itself, food?

text.NRSV: Is wisdom with the aged, and understanding in length of days?
text.Rotherham: In the Ancient, is wisdom, and [in] Length of Days, understanding:

text.NRSV: “With God are wisdom and might; he has counsel and understanding.
text.Rotherham: With Him, are wisdom and strength, to Him, pertain counsel and understanding.

text.NRSV: If he tears down, no one can rebuild; if he imprisons, no one can set free.
text.Rotherham: Lo! He pulleth down, and it cannot be built, He closeth up over a man, and it cannot be opened:

text.NRSV: If he withholds the waters, they dry up; if he sends them out, they overwhelm the land.
text.Rotherham: Lo! He holdeth back the waters, and they dry up, or sendeth them out, and they transform the earth:

text.NRSV: With him are strength and wisdom; the deceived and the deceiver are his.
text.Rotherham: With Him, is strength and effective wisdom, to Him, belong he that erreth, and he that causeth to err.

text.NRSV: He leads counselors away stripped, and makes fools of judges.
text.Rotherham: Who leadeth away counsellors [as] a spoil, and, judges, He befooleth:

text.NRSV: He looses the sash of kings, and binds a waistcloth on their loins.
text.Rotherham: The fetters of kings, He looseth, or hath bound a slave’s waistcloth about their loins:

text.NRSV: He leads priests away stripped, and overthrows the mighty.
text.Rotherham: Who leadeth away priests [as] a spoil, and, men firmly seated, He overturneth:

text.NRSV: He deprives of speech those who are trusted, and takes away the discernment of the elders.
text.Rotherham: Setting aside the speech of the trusty, and, the discernment of elders, He taketh away:

text.NRSV: He pours contempt on princes, and looses the belt of the strong.
text.Rotherham: Pouring contempt upon nobles, and, the girdle of the mighty, hath He loosed:

text.NRSV: He uncovers the deeps out of darkness, and brings deep darkness to light.
text.Rotherham: Laying open deep things, out of darkness, and bringing out to light, the death-shade:

text.NRSV: He makes nations great, then destroys them; he enlarges nations, then leads them away.
text.Rotherham: Who giveth greatness to nations, or destroyeth them, Who spreadeth out nations, or leadeth them into exile:

text.NRSV: He strips understanding from the leaders of the earth, and makes them wander in a pathless waste.
text.Rotherham: Who taketh away the sense of the chiefs of the people of the earth, and hath caused them to wander in a pathless waste:

text.NRSV: They grope in the dark without light; he makes them stagger like a drunkard.
text.Rotherham: They grope about in the dark, having no light, and He hath made them to reel, like a drunken man.

text.NRSV: “Look, my eye has seen all this, my ear has heard and understood it.
text.Rotherham: Lo! all [this], hath mine own eye seen,—Mine ear hath heard and understood it:

text.NRSV: What you know, I also know; I am not inferior to you.
text.Rotherham: Just as ye know, I too, know, I, fall not short, of you.

text.NRSV: But I would speak to the Almighty, and I desire to argue my case with God.
text.Rotherham: But indeed, I, unto the Almighty, would speak, and, to direct my argument unto GOD, would I be well pleased.

text.NRSV: As for you, you whitewash with lies; all of you are worthless physicians.
text.Rotherham: For, in truth, ye, do besmear with falsehood, Worthless physicians, all of you!

text.NRSV: If you would only keep silent, that would be your wisdom!
text.Rotherham: Oh that ye would, altogether hold your peace, and it should serve you for wisdom!

text.NRSV: Hear now my reasoning, and listen to the pleadings of my lips.
text.Rotherham: Hear, I pray you, the argument of my mouth, and, to the pleadings of my lips, give heed:—

text.NRSV: Will you speak falsely for God, and speak deceitfully for him?
text.Rotherham: Is it, for God, ye would speak perversely? And, for him, would ye speak deceit:

text.NRSV: Will you show partiality toward him, will you plead the case for God?
text.Rotherham: Even, for him, would ye be partial? Or, for GOD, would ye [so] plead?

text.NRSV: Will it be well with you when he searches you out? Or can you deceive him, as one person deceives another?
text.Rotherham: Would it be well, when he searched you out? Or, as one might jest with a mortal, would ye jest, with him?

text.NRSV: He will surely rebuke you if in secret you show partiality.
text.Rotherham: He will, severely rebuke, you, if ye are secretly partial.

text.NRSV: Will not his majesty terrify you, and the dread of him fall upon you?
text.Rotherham: Shall not, his majesty, overwhelm you? and, the dread of him, fall upon you?

text.NRSV: Your maxims are proverbs of ashes, your defenses are defenses of clay.
text.Rotherham: Are not your memorable sayings, proverbs of ashes? Breastworks of clay, your breastworks?

text.NRSV: “Let me have silence, and I will speak, and let come on me what may.
text.Rotherham: Quietly let me alone, that, I, may speak out, then let come on me, what may.

text.NRSV: I will take my flesh in my teeth, and put my life in my hand.
text.Rotherham: In any case, I will take up my flesh in my teeth, and, my life, will I put in my hand:

text.NRSV: See, he will kill me; I have no hope; but I will defend my ways to his face.
text.Rotherham: Lo! he may slay me, [yet], for him, will I wait,—Nevertheless, my ways—unto his face, will I show to be right:

text.NRSV: This will be my salvation, that the godless shall not come before him.
text.Rotherham: Even he, will be on my side—unto salvation, For, not before his face, shall any impious person come.

text.NRSV: Listen carefully to my words, and let my declaration be in your ears.
text.Rotherham: Hear ye patiently my speech, and be my declaration in your ears.

text.NRSV: I have indeed prepared my case; I know that I shall be vindicated.
text.Rotherham: Lo! I pray you, I have set forth in order a plea, I know that, I, shall be found right.

text.NRSV: Who is there that will contend with me? For then I would be silent and die.
text.Rotherham: Who is it that shall contend with me? For, now, if I should hold my peace, why! I should breathe my last!

text.NRSV: Only grant two things to me, then I will not hide myself from your face:
text.Rotherham: Only, two things, do thou not with me, then, from thy face, will I not hide me:—

text.NRSV: withdraw your hand far from me, and do not let dread of you terrify me.
text.Rotherham: Thy hand—from off me, take thou far away, and, thy terror, let it not startle me!

text.NRSV: Then call, and I will answer; or let me speak, and you reply to me.
text.Rotherham: Then call thou, and, I, will answer, Or I will speak, and reply thou unto me.

text.NRSV: How many are my iniquities and my sins? Make me know my transgression and my sin.
text.Rotherham: How many are mine iniquities and sins? My transgression and my sin, let me know!

text.NRSV: Why do you hide your face, and count me as your enemy?
text.Rotherham: Wherefore, thy face, shouldst thou hide? Or count me, as an enemy to thee?

text.NRSV: Will you frighten a settled leaf and pursue dry chaff?
text.Rotherham: A driven leaf, wilt thou cause to tremble? Or, dry stubble, wilt thou pursue?

text.NRSV: For you write bitter things against me, and make me inherit the iniquities of my youth.
text.Rotherham: For thou writest, against me, bitter things, and dost make me inherit the iniquities of my youth;

text.NRSV: You put my feet in the stocks, and watch all my paths; you set a bound to the soles of my feet.
text.Rotherham: And thou dost put—in the stocks—my feet, and observest all my paths, Against the roots of my feet, thou dost cut out a bound;

text.NRSV: One wastes away like a rotten thing, like a garment that is moth-eaten.”
text.Rotherham: And, a man himself, as a rotten thing, weareth out, as a garment which the moth hath eaten.

text.NRSV: “A mortal, born of woman, few of days and full of trouble,
text.Rotherham: Man that is born of a woman, is of few days, and full of trouble:

text.NRSV: comes up like a flower and withers, flees like a shadow and does not last.
text.Rotherham: As a flower, he cometh forth—and fadeth, He fleeth also as a shadow, and continueth not.

text.NRSV: Do you fix your eyes on such a one? Do you bring me into judgment with you?
text.Rotherham: And yet upon such a one as this, hast thou opened thine eye? And, him, wouldst thou bring into judgment with thee?

text.NRSV: Who can bring a clean thing out of an unclean? No one can.
text.Rotherham: Who can bring a clean thing out of an unclean? Not one!

text.NRSV: Since their days are determined, and the number of their months is known to you, and you have appointed the bounds that they cannot pass,
text.Rotherham: If determined am his days, the number of his months, is with thee, Fixed times for him, thou hast appointed and he cannot go beyond.

text.NRSV: look away from them, and desist, that they may enjoy, like laborers, their days.
text.Rotherham: Look sway from him, that he may rest, Till he shall pay off, as a hireling, his day.

text.NRSV: “For there is hope for a tree, if it is cut down, that it will sprout again, and that its shoots will not cease.
text.Rotherham: Though there is—for a tree—hope,—if it should be cut down, that, again, it will grow, and, the tender branch thereof, will not cease;

text.NRSV: Though its root grows old in the earth, and its stump dies in the ground,
text.Rotherham: If its root, should become old in the earth, and, in the dust, its stock should die:

text.NRSV: yet at the scent of water it will bud and put forth branches like a young plant.
text.Rotherham: Through the scent of water, it may break forth, and produce branches like a sapling,

text.NRSV: But mortals die, and are laid low; humans expire, and where are they?
text.Rotherham: Yet, man, dieth, and is prostrate, Yea the son of earth doth cease to breathe, and where is he?

text.NRSV: As waters fail from a lake, and a river wastes away and dries up,
text.Rotherham: Waters, have failed from, the sea, and, a river, may waste and dry up;

text.NRSV: so mortals lie down and do not rise again; until the heavens are no more, they will not awake or be roused out of their sleep.
text.Rotherham: So, a man, hath lain down, and shall not arise, until there are no heavens, they shall not awake, nor be roused up out of their sleep.

text.NRSV: O that you would hide me in Sheol, that you would conceal me until your wrath is past, that you would appoint me a set time, and remember me!
text.Rotherham: Oh that, in hades, thou wouldst hide me! that thou wouldst keep me secret, until the turn of thine anger, that thou wouldst set for me a fixed time, and remember me:

text.NRSV: If mortals die, will they live again? All the days of my service I would wait until my release should come.
text.Rotherham: If a man die, can he live again? All the days of my warfare, would I wait, until my relief should come:—

text.NRSV: You would call, and I would answer you; you would long for the work of your hands.
text.Rotherham: Thou shouldst call, and, I, would answer thee,—For the work of thine own hand, thou shouldst long.

text.NRSV: For then you would not number my steps; you would not keep watch over my sin;
text.Rotherham: For, now, my steps, thou countest, Thou wilt not pass over my sin:

text.NRSV: my transgression would be sealed up in a bag, and you would cover over my iniquity.
text.Rotherham: Sealed up in a bag, is my transgression, and thou hast glued over mine iniquity.

text.NRSV: “But the mountain falls and crumbles away, and the rock is removed from its place;
text.Rotherham: But, in very deed, a mountain falling, will lie prostrate, or, a rock moved out of its place:

text.NRSV: the waters wear away the stones; the torrents wash away the soil of the earth; so you destroy the hope of mortals.
text.Rotherham: Stones, have been hollowed out by waters, the floods thereof wash away the dust of the earth, and, the hope of mortal man, thou hast destroyed:

text.NRSV: You prevail forever against them, and they pass away; you change their countenance, and send them away.
text.Rotherham: Thou dost overpower him utterly, and he departeth, Disfiguring his face, so, hast thou sent him away.

text.NRSV: Their children come to honor, and they do not know it; they are brought low, and it is not perceived.
text.Rotherham: His sons, come to honour, and he knoweth it not, Or they are brought low, and he perceiveth it not of them.

text.NRSV: They feel only the pain of their own bodies, and mourn only for themselves.”
text.Rotherham: But, his flesh, for himself, is in pain, and, his soul, for himself, doth mourn.

text.NRSV: Then Eliphaz the Temanite answered:
text.Rotherham: Then responded Eliphaz the Temanite, and said:

text.NRSV: “Should the wise answer with windy knowledge, and fill themselves with the east wind?
text.Rotherham: Should, a wise man, answer unreal knowledge? or fill, with the east wind, his inner man?

text.NRSV: Should they argue in words that do not profit, or in speeches that do no good?
text.Rotherham: Disputing with discourse that doth no good, or with speech, wherein is no profit?

text.NRSV: But you are doing away with the fear of God, and hindering meditation before God.
text.Rotherham: But, thou, wouldst take away reverence, and wouldst attain unto meditation before GOD.

text.NRSV: For your iniquity teaches your mouth, and you choose the tongue of the crafty.
text.Rotherham: For thine own mouth would teach thine iniquity, and thou wouldst choose the tongue of the crafty.

text.NRSV: Your own mouth condemns you, and not I; your own lips testify against you.
text.Rotherham: Thine own mouth shall condemn thee, and, not I, And, thine own lips, shall testify against thee.

text.NRSV: “Are you the firstborn of the human race? Were you brought forth before the hills?
text.Rotherham: The first of mankind, wast thou born? Or, before the hills, wast thou brought forth?

text.NRSV: Have you listened in the council of God? And do you limit wisdom to yourself?
text.Rotherham: In the secret council of GOD, hast thou been wont to hearken? Or canst thou attain for thyself unto wisdom?

text.NRSV: What do you know that we do not know? What do you understand that is not clear to us?
text.Rotherham: What knowest thou, that we know not? [What] understandest thou, and the same, is not with us?

text.NRSV: The gray-haired and the aged are on our side, those older than your father.
text.Rotherham: Both hoary and venerable, are among us, one mightier than thy father in days!

text.NRSV: Are the consolations of God too small for you, or the word that deals gently with you?
text.Rotherham: Too small for thee, are the consolations of GOD? or a word spoken gently with thee?

text.NRSV: Why does your heart carry you away, and why do your eyes flash,
text.Rotherham: How doth thine own heart carry thee away, and how thine eyes do roll!

text.NRSV: so that you turn your spirit against God, and let such words go out of your mouth?
text.Rotherham: For thy spirit, replieth against GOD, and thou bringest forth—out of thy mouth—words!

text.NRSV: What are mortals, that they can be clean? Or those born of woman, that they can be righteous?
text.Rotherham: What is a mortal, that he should be pure? or that righteous should be one born of a woman?

text.NRSV: God puts no trust even in his holy ones, and the heavens are not clean in his sight;
text.Rotherham: Lo! in his holy ones, he putteth not confidence, and, the heavens, are not pure in his eyes:

text.NRSV: how much less one who is abominable and corrupt, one who drinks iniquity like water!
text.Rotherham: How much less when one is detested and corrupt, a man who drinketh in—like water—perversity.

text.NRSV: “I will show you; listen to me; what I have seen I will declare—
text.Rotherham: I will tell thee—hear me, Since this I have seen, I must needs declare it.

text.NRSV: what sages have told, and their ancestors have not hidden,
text.Rotherham: Which, wise men, tell, and deny not [that which is] from their fathers.

text.NRSV: to whom alone the land was given, and no stranger passed among them.
text.Rotherham: To them alone, was the earth given, and no alien passed through their midst:

text.NRSV: The wicked writhe in pain all their days, through all the years that are laid up for the ruthless.
text.Rotherham: All the days of the lawless man, he, doth writhe with pain, and, the number of years, is hidden from the tyrant;

text.NRSV: Terrifying sounds are in their ears; in prosperity the destroyer will come upon them.
text.Rotherham: A noise of dreadful things, is in his ears, In prosperity, the destroyer cometh upon him;

text.NRSV: They do not believe that they will return out of darkness, and they are destined for the sword.
text.Rotherham: He hath no confidence to come back out of darkness, he, being destined to the power oft the sword;

text.NRSV: They wander abroad for bread, saying, ‘Where is it?’ They know that a day of darkness is ready at hand;
text.Rotherham: A wanderer, he, for bread, [saying] Where [is it] ? He knoweth that, prepared by his own hand, is the day of darkness;

text.NRSV: distress and anguish terrify them; they prevail against them, like a king prepared for battle.
text.Rotherham: Distress and anguish shall startle him, It shall overpower him, like a king ready for the onset:

text.NRSV: Because they stretched out their hands against God, and bid defiance to the Almighty,
text.Rotherham: Because he had stretched out—against GOD—his hand, and, against the Almighty, had been wont to behave himself proudly;

text.NRSV: running stubbornly against him with a thick-bossed shield;
text.Rotherham: He used to run against him with uplifted neck, with the stout bosses of his bucklers;

text.NRSV: because they have covered their faces with their fat, and gathered fat upon their loins,
text.Rotherham: For he had covered his face with his fatness, and had gathered a superabundance on his loins;

text.NRSV: they will live in desolate cities, in houses that no one should inhabit, which are destined to become heaps of ruins;
text.Rotherham: And had inhabited demolished cities, houses, wherein men would not dwell, that were destined to become heaps.

text.NRSV: they will not be rich, and their wealth will not endure, nor will they strike root in the earth;
text.Rotherham: He shall not be rich, nor shall his substance continue, neither shall their shadow stretch along on the earth;

text.NRSV: they will not escape from darkness; the flame will wither their shoots, and their blossom will be swept away by the wind.
text.Rotherham: He shall not depart out of darkness, his young branch, shall the flame dry up, and he shall depart, by the breath of his own mouth!

text.NRSV: Let them not trust in emptiness, deceiving themselves; for emptiness will be their recompense.
text.Rotherham: Let no one trust in him that—by vanity—is deceived, for, vanity, shall be his recompense;

text.NRSV: It will be paid in full before their time, and their branch will not be green.
text.Rotherham: Before his day, shall it be accomplished, with, his palm-top, not covered with leaves;

text.NRSV: They will shake off their unripe grape, like the vine, and cast off their blossoms, like the olive tree.
text.Rotherham: He shall wrong—like a vine—his sour grapes, and shall cast off—as an olive-tree—his blossom.

text.NRSV: For the company of the godless is barren, and fire consumes the tents of bribery.
text.Rotherham: For, the family of the impious, is unfruitful, and, a fire, hath devoured the tents of bribery;

text.NRSV: They conceive mischief and bring forth evil and their heart prepares deceit.”
text.Rotherham: Conceiving mischief, and bringing forth iniquity, yea, their inmost soul, prepareth deceit.

text.NRSV: Then Job answered:
text.Rotherham: Then responded Job, and said:—

text.NRSV: “I have heard many such things; miserable comforters are you all.
text.Rotherham: I have heard many such things, Wearisome comforters, are ye all!

text.NRSV: Have windy words no limit? Or what provokes you that you keep on talking?
text.Rotherham: Is there to be an end to windy words? Or what so strongly exciteth thee, that thou must respond?

text.NRSV: I also could talk as you do, if you were in my place; I could join words together against you, and shake my head at you.
text.Rotherham: I also, like you, could speak,—If your soul were in the place of my soul, I could string together words against you, and could therewith shake over you my head.

text.NRSV: I could fortify you with my mouth, and the solace of my lips would assuage your pain.
text.Rotherham: I could make you determined, by my mouth, and then my lip-solace should restrain you.

text.NRSV: “If I speak, my pain is not assuaged, and if I forbear, how much of it leaves me?
text.Rotherham: Though I do speak, unassuaged is my stinging pain,—And, if I forbear, of what am I relieved?

text.NRSV: Surely now God has worn me out; he has made desolate all my company.
text.Rotherham: But, now, hath he wearied me, thou hast destroyed all my family;

text.NRSV: And he has shriveled me up, which has been a witness against me; my leanness has risen up against me, and it testifies to my face.
text.Rotherham: And, having captured me, it hath served, as a witness; and so my wasting away hath risen up against me, in my face, it answereth.
